author: @PossumReviews
text: The reason they needed so much light was because the Technicolor process required a special camera that recorded on three strips of film simultaneously. Light would pass through the lens and be split by a prism before passing through three colored filters onto three strips of film which would later be combined together to make a full color picture. Splitting the light three ways meant each strip got a third of the light, so they needed three times as much light on set to compensate, and the carbon arc lamps they were using at the time were extremely hot.

replies.author: @shadowbyname2802
replies.text:
Yeah, in the years since hearing these stories, I’ll admit it’s impacted my enjoyment of the movie. I already wasn’t much of a fan of it in the first place. While I do respect its place and enjoy classic movies/films, I’ve never been a big musical person. Combine that with the Witch scarring me at the age of 2 for a decade, I never really learned to love this movie so many others did.

As I got older I heard more of the stories and learned some of the more realistic fears in the world. One of which included the apathy of those in power, and it ran rampant here. There was so much unnecessary suffering endured on this movie, and I blame much of it on the executives and directors for allowing it to happen. It was the early days, I understand things like 10:58  conditions and actor’s rights weren’t around. It doesn’t change the fact that it happened however, and although I respect this movie’s place in history, I can’t watch it without wondering how many of the performances were acting or real.

text: I'm one of your oldest fans, and can remember in the 50s and 60s, familys would mark their calendar for the date and time that Wizard of Oz would be presented on television.   It became an annual event.   There was no instant access, or DVDs or even VHS cassettes yet.   You talked about it with friends in school!   You waited for the airing day:  "Tonight is the Wizard of Oz!!"    It's strange to look back on, as I've seen a huge Arc in technology in just 80 years. Great review of an amazing production.   Thanks Drinker.

author: @NeuroticCollegeChick
text: A bit more about Margaret Hamilton: she was, despite the abuse she took from the studio after her accident, a lovely woman according to everyone who knew her. She got asked to keep reprising her wicked witch role for years after Oz, which likely hurt her career. Her scars plus her affiliation with the role led to her being typecast as a villain for several roles, to the point where she went out of her way to look for roles that were kind, gentle, or comedic characters. Children would recognize her as the evil witch and be frightened of her, which hurt her because she loved kids and said if she wasn't an actress she wanted to be a teacher. There's even an episode of Mr. Rogers from the 70s where she's a guest to explain that the wicked witch is just a character.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: @MarkSmith-nw4os Here in the Uk it was Christmas.  It was first shown on tv in the early to mid 70s I forget the exact year. I was a tot at the time. And my parents and I all sat and watched it and I was mesmerised. I thought it the most incredible film and lovely story and it became what I thought of as a child as top quality. Of course the tv companies then went on to show it every Christmas from that point on. Some years it was Christmas eve or Boxing Day or New Year. And it went from an event to expected, to people getting sick of the sight of it by about 1980. Back then first run films were a big deal, in the Christmas season and kept for that. Films like Poseidon Adventure, Towering Inferno, Where Eagles Dare and the Great Escape all appeared first at Christmas in the UK and went on to be slotted in as needed for decades after. I got old enough to understand Wizard of Oz, then older still and started to think it uncool. Then as an adult with so many film choices I no longer watched musicals...but it was still being shown over Christmas or Easter till the had the choice of more movies as more had been made by then and it gradually fell out of fashion by the mid 1990s....I have not seen it myself for years now. One Christmas, maybe this year I will rewatch it for old times sake...

replies.author: @bigmclargehuge3653
replies.text:
Margaret Hamilton was a kindergarten teacher, so she felt really guilty when she realized that her character was frightening children. She disowned the role and she quit acting all together because of it, even refusing interviews and offers to do conventions.

Years later, Mr. Rogers was the only one who could successfully talk her into wearing the hat again... under the promise that she could explain to kids that it was only make believe. She was so deeply touched by his kindness that she remained personal friends with Fred Rogers, and the other cast members or the rest of her life... and she visited all of them in the hospital when their children were born.

replies.author: @cattysplat
replies.text: @writerpatrick People forget film grain was all over every movie, the older the movie the more the grain, baked right into the frame on set with dust on physical film. Then you had even more grain in every individual film reel, the projector, all the smoke wafting around the room from everyone smoking, also coating the projector screen. Then for TV you had analogue transmission which always had some haze, especially if the signal wavered or badly tuned, the warping and shrinking of the picture on a small curved 4:3 box CRT screen. Even VHS tape had dust on the tape and reader heads, with tracking issues and warped tape over time.

replies.author: @EVAUnit4A
replies.text:
@richardsanchez5444  I'm not going to bash on it because I still dearly treasure it, but as someone who saw it in the theater as a child when it debuted, Disney's _The Little Mermaid_ (1989) had some production hiccups as well, leading it to the point where it became a question of whether it would make it to the finish line either!

-  This one is perhaps the _best-known_ speed bump, but the entire "Part of Your World" song sequence was frowned upon by the Disney executives, because they thought it was boring.  It took directors Ron Clements and John Musker fighting like hell to keep the full "PoYW" sequence intact!  _If_ the executives had gotten their way, _thee only time_ that we would have heard Ariel sing for us would have been the morning after she rescued Prince Eric- "Part of Your World (Reprise)".  Hindsight being 20/20 (and again, I will not bash the movie), over the course of 35 years "PoYW" has become iconic, but a small number of occasions I have heard people tell me that the song is "boring" or lacking dynamics... just goes to show that _maybe_ those Disney execs had a point after all.
-  Much of the visual effects (lightning, fire, magic, etc.) were completed by an east Asian studio (I forgot which country- SKorea or Taiwan), and one particular shipment of the then-obscene-amount of finished _bubble_ animation got delayed due to a cargo ship getting caught-up in a typhoon.
-  This next one is (and I cannot emphasize this word enough) _rumored,_ but I have not been able to nail down after 35 years.  _Apparently,_ actress Jodi Benson (Ariel) was 'difficult to work with', and walked-out towards the end of her recordings.  _It is speculated_ that the reason Ariel does not say or do anything of importance after the giant Ursula appears is because they had to rewrite the last scenes of the movie without the required dialogue from Benson!  (Hell, even second-wave feminists back in 1989 picked-up on Ariel's peculiar silence and inaction, though without the internet they may not have known the reasons _why._ )  Considering Benson would return to voice Ariel in two made-for-video movies, a TV series, several video games and other out-of-continuity appearances (as well as voicing Tour Guide Barbie for _Toy Story 2_ ), it's safe to assume the _rumored_ difficulties were dealt with fairly quickly... which is why _I doubt_ whether the rumor is true-or-not.

Tough competition:

The Wizard Of Oz: Toxic asbestos snow, 2 failed fire stunts involving a witch vanishing into smoke, Judy Garland fed diet pills and stimulants to get through her scenes, the cowardly lion actor on a diet of soup and milkshakes because he cant remove his 90 pound real fur suit, makeup for the 2 tin man actors literally causing medical emergencies, the dog accidentally got stepped on once, horny little people actors engaging in orgies and heavy drinking on their downtime, name-calling Garland a fat piggy because she's still not slim enough, the scarecrow actor suffered facial scarring wearing the mask for a long time, actually using butterfly nets to catch the small dwarf actors, Margaret Hamilton enduring green skin for several months from the witch makeup and literally trying to get actors and actresses back on set mere days after accidents sent them to hospital

replies.author: @GreyWolfLeaderTW
replies.text: While we know today how dangerous asbestos is, before the 1930s the nature of the health hazard the material posed wasn't widely known, and it wasn't until thorough scientific studies in the late 1930s and early 1940s were conducted that the full danger of the material was revealed. It would take nearly 30 years after that for asbestos to drop off in use as healthier alternatives were found. Use of asbestos by humans for various purposes (including insulation against heat) actually dates back thousands of years. Asbestos is still used for industrial functions today.

replies.text: @GdSza My understanding is that it depends very much on the type of asbestos used. Threads that are long enough or short enough don't really cause problems, just those that are of exactly the length that are small enough to be breathed into the longs and large enough to get stuck there. Unfortunately, those were the bits often used for insulation.

replies.author: @HappyTribble
replies.text: Ex-Industrial Hygienist here. The dangers of Asbestos were known dating back to the Roman times (slaves forced to mine Asbestos were effectively given a death sentence) but nothing was as good as Asbestos was in terms of improving anything it was mixed with. It improved wear life for tiles, fireproofing, insulation, etc. The dangers were well known but heavily downplayed for reasons listed above. Plus a lag time of 30+ years to kill you (take or give a few years depending on the type of exposure) meant you could use it with impunity, secure in the knowledge the deaths would never be linked to its use.

replies.author: @OmegaPaladin144

Transite countertops and incredibly durable -  as long as you don't break out power tools on them, they are not breaking.  I think it was only very recently we moved away from asbestos brake pads

author: @ZamboniZone
text:
There was actually another director in between The First guy, and Flemming. He agreed to stay and clean up the production until they got a new director. He was the one who got rid of Dorothy's blonde wig and makeup.

As for the slap. According to one of those anniversary behind the scenes books. The slap was for a different reason. Apparently Burt Lar's performance when Dorothy smacked the Lion would cause Judy to bust out laughing and she would laugh for about ten minutes. So between take, Flemming took her behind a tree and slapped her so she would stay calm. And even then she only just barely managed it.
       Also there's a book, most likely out of print called "the Munchkins remember, the Wizard of Oz." It's a fascinating read as the author tracked down information on all the munchkin actors, as they are only credited in the movie as, the munchkins. And he interviewed a few that were still alive. According to some of them, while there were some issues, with one actor having to be fired for stalking his ex wife, there were no drunken orgies. Also, none of the Munchkins hung themselves on set. Sorry not sorry

author: @richardthefox3412
text: Minor correction but the Wizard of Oz wasn’t the first feature film to be made in Color, or even three strip Technicolor. The first to beads in that formate was 1935’s Becky Sharp, and the first to be made in color was the Kinecolor Documentary “ With Our King and Queen Through India” in 1912, albeit only 10 minutes survive. The first Hollywood (though not American) made color feature was 1922’s “Toll of the Sea.” No question however that this film was still a major technical achievement at the time, despite the hellish production.

author: @shadrakistann6833
text: I became a fan of Bert Larh back in high school when I played the cowardly lion. I watched so many of his performances for this role, since his portrayal was simply a piece of Americana I wanted to do justice to. I know the experiences are not comparable but I've since felt a kinship to him because of how difficult the costume and makeup was. The costume was borrowed from a professional troop and was hard to physically act in and harder to breathe in because of the perfume it was drenched in. The previous performers drenched it in sweat and I was no acception. On top of that, the first makeup artist I had did such a poor job that I have a facial scar to this day from the make up. I petitioned for a new artist who did an amazing job and eventually became my wife several years later. My appreciation for this film will never waiver but I do have a separate and unshakable appreciation for the actors and actresses who brought it to life.
